definition: A period of reduced funding and interest in artificial intelligence research, typically due to unmet expectations and slow progress. Notable AI winters occurred in the 1970s and late 1980s/early 1990s.
A deep convolutional neural network developed by Krizhevsky, Sutskever, and Hinton that won the ImageNet competition in 2012, demonstrating the power of deep learning for computer vision.
An AI system developed by DeepMind that achieved a breakthrough in protein structure prediction, solving a long-standing challenge in biology.
A computer program developed by DeepMind that defeated world champion Go player Lee Sedol in 2016, demonstrating the power of reinforcement learning and deep neural networks.
A 1966 report by the US government that criticized the progress of machine translation, leading to reduced funding for AI research.
A family of large language models developed by Anthropic, contributing to the generative AI boom in the 2020s.
A mathematical model inspired by biological neurons, first described by McCulloch and Pitts in 1943, forming the basis of neural networks.
An algorithm for training multi-layer neural networks by propagating errors backward through the network, popularized by Rumelhart, Hinton, and Williams in 1986.
A probabilistic graphical model that represents a set of variables and their conditional dependencies, popularized by Judea Pearl in 1988.
A type of stochastic recurrent neural network introduced by Geoffrey Hinton and Terry Sejnowski in 1985.
A conversational AI developed by OpenAI, based on the GPT-3.5 and GPT-4 large language models, launched in 2022 and widely adopted for its advanced language capabilities.
A class of deep neural networks, most commonly applied to analyzing visual imagery. Yann LeCun pioneered their use for digit recognition in the 1990s.
A long-term artificial intelligence project started by Douglas Lenat in 1984 to assemble a comprehensive ontology and knowledge base of common sense knowledge.
A series of competitions for autonomous vehicles, sponsored by the US Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency (DARPA), with the first successful completion in 2005.
A chess-playing computer developed by IBM that defeated world chess champion Garry Kasparov in 1997.
A subset of machine learning involving neural networks with many layers, enabling breakthroughs in image, speech, and text processing.
An early natural language processing computer program created by Joseph Weizenbaum in 1966, simulating conversation and laying the foundation for chatbots.
A computer system that emulates the decision-making ability of a human expert, using rule-based logic. Examples include MYCIN and XCON.
A Japanese government initiative launched in 1982 to create computers using massively parallel computing and logic programming.
A universal problem-solving algorithm developed by Newell and Simon in 1957, influential in AI planning and search.
A large language model developed by Google, launched in 2023 as a major competitor in the generative AI space.

Generative Pre-trained Transformer (GPT) is a family of large language models developed by OpenAI. GPT models are trained on vast amounts of text data using unsupervised learning and leverage the Transformer architecture to generate human-like text for a wide range of tasks.
A form of recurrent neural network introduced by John Hopfield in 1982, used for associative memory and optimization problems.
An AI system developed by IBM that defeated human champions on the quiz show Jeopardy! in 2011, showcasing advances in natural language processing.
A large-scale image database launched in 2009 by Fei-Fei Li and colleagues, used for visual object recognition research.
A 1973 report by James Lighthill that criticized AI research in the UK, leading to funding cuts and the first AI winter in Britain.
A type of computer designed to efficiently run the Lisp programming language, popular in AI research in the 1980s before the AI winter.
A computer program developed by Allen Newell and Herbert A. Simon in 1955 that proved mathematical theorems, considered the first AI program.
A subset of artificial intelligence in which systems learn patterns from data to make predictions or decisions without being explicitly programmed for every task.
The observation by Hans Moravec that high-level reasoning requires little computation, but low-level sensorimotor skills require enormous computational resources.
In 2024, Nobel Prizes were awarded to John Hopfield, Geoffrey Hinton, Demis Hassabis, John Jumper, and David Baker for their contributions to AI and protein folding.
An early neural network model invented by Frank Rosenblatt in 1957, capable of learning simple patterns.
A 1969 book by Marvin Minsky and Seymour Papert that analyzed the limitations of single-layer neural networks, contributing to the first AI winter.
An approach to AI based on encoding human knowledge as rules and logic (if this, then that).
Making AI models larger and training them on more data, which has been shown to produce unexpectedly better results and new capabilities.
The Stochastic Neural Analog Reinforcement Calculator, built by Marvin Minsky and Dean Edmonds in 1951, was the first artificial neural network machine.

A neural network architecture introduced in the paper 'Attention Is All You Need' (2017) by Vaswani et al. It relies entirely on self-attention mechanisms, dispensing with recurrence and convolutions, and has become the foundation for most modern large language models.
A theoretical computational model introduced by Alan Turing in 1936, which manipulates symbols on a strip of tape according to a set of rules. It is a foundational concept in computer science and AI.
A test proposed by Alan Turing in 1950 to determine whether a machine can exhibit intelligent behavior indistinguishable from a human.
